system_prompt:
You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — tritone-substitution-licks

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

This book teaches tritone substitution as a practical soloing tool for jazz guitar, structured around a single canonical progression—the II V I realized as Dm7 | Db7 | CM7—and built outward from that foundation into a reusable, transposable framework. The pedagogical arc moves from concept to application: the master first establishes the substitution principle (replacing a V7 chord with a dom7 chord a tritone away), then systematically maps every available soloing option over the substitute chord back to its harmonic function on the original dominant.

Chapter 1 introduces the book's core analytical method: four soloing options over the substituted dominant—dom7 arpeggio, dom9 arpeggio, major pentatonic scale, and Mixolydian mode—each subjected to tone-mapping analysis that identifies exactly which intervals of the substitute chord correspond to which altered extensions (b9, #11, b13) of the original V7. This interval-mapping procedure is the master's characteristic move: every lick is deconstructed measure-by-measure and every scale degree is assigned a harmonic label, so the student understands not just what to play but why it works.

The book's terminology is precise and load-bearing. Tritone substitution, the four soloing options, the altered tones (b9, #11, b13), approach notes, and the Dorian bebop scale (defined as the Dorian mode with an added chromatic passing tone) are all introduced as reusable technical vocabulary. These terms are not decorative—they anchor the tone-mapping analysis and make the framework portable across keys.

The master's prescribed method is explicitly analytical rather than intuitive: learn the substitution principle, identify the substitute chord, apply one of the four soloing options, trace each tone back to its altered-extension function on the original dominant, and then transpose the entire framework to new keys. The Dorian bebop scale receives specific attention as a soloing tool over minor chords in the II V I context, extending the harmonic vocabulary beyond the dominant substitution itself. The overall pedagogical stance is that understanding the intervallic logic of substitution—not merely memorizing licks—is what allows the student to generate material independently in any key.

# Chapter 1 — Tritone Substitution Guitar Licks

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chapter 1 teaches tritone substitution as a technique for soloing over jazz progressions, specifically demonstrating how to replace a V7 chord with another 7th chord a tritone away to target altered tones like b9, #11, and b13. The chapter establishes four primary options for soloing over the substituted dominant—dom7 arpeggio, dom9 arpeggio, major pentatonic scale, and Mixolydian mode—each with detailed tone-mapping analysis showing which intervals of the substitute chord correspond to which altered extensions of the original dominant. The master's characteristic approach is methodical and analytical: each lick is deconstructed measure-by-measure, and every scale degree is mapped to its harmonic function, building a reusable framework that students can transpose to any key. Load-bearing terminology introduced includes tritone substitution itself, the four soloing options, altered tones (b9, #11, b13), approach notes, and the Dorian bebop scale (Dorian mode with a chromatic passing tone), all anchored to the canonical II V I progression and its Dm7 | Db7 | CM7 realization.

# Chapter 1 — Tritone Substitution Guitar Licks

_Pages 1-5._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Tritone substitution definition

> The basic application of a
> tritone chord substitution is to take any 7th chord and play another 7th chord that has its root a tritone away
> from the original.

**Significance (p. 1).** This is the foundational definition of the technique the entire chapter is built around — without it, none of the subsequent applications make sense.

## Tritone sub over II V I

> This guitar lesson demonstrates how you can play scales and arpeggios starting from the b5
> (a tritone away) of the V7 chord in a II V I chord progression. This way you will highlight altered tones as the
> b9 and the #11.

**Significance (p. 1).** This passage establishes the core method — using the substitution to target specific altered tones — which governs every lick and scale choice that follows.

## II V I tritone sub example

> Let’s take a C major II VI chord progression Dm7 | G7 | CM7 | % | After applying a tritone substitution to
> the V7 chord of a II V I chord progression, we get the following sequence Dm7 | Db7 | CM7 | % | The idea
> behind is to play Db over G7.

**Significance (p. 1).** This is the canonical worked example that concretely applies the abstract definition to a specific progression, giving the reader a template they can transpose to any key.

## Dominant 7 arpeggio tone mapping

> The root of Db7 highlights the #11/b5 of G7.

**Significance (p. 1).** This passage maps each tone of the Db7 arpeggio to its function within G7, teaching the student exactly which altered colors are produced by option one.

## Dominant 9 arpeggio option

> The second option is to play a dominant 9 . .
> arpeggio (1 - 3-5 -b7-9) which means that Dominant 9 Arpeggio Shapes
> you have to add the ninth to the previous 3 0 a ® ® 9
> dom7 arpeggio.

**Significance (p. 1).** Introduces the extension from dom7 to dom9 as the incremental step for option two, adding the b13 color to the palette available over G7.

## Major pentatonic scale formula

> The third option consists in playing a major
> pentatonic scale (1 - 2 - 3-5-6).

**Significance (p. 1).** Defines the major pentatonic formula as option three, anchoring subsequent tone analysis to a specific scale formula.

## Bb as approach note rule

> The sixth (Bb) of Db7 should be used as an
> approach note in order to target a chord tone

**Significance (p. 1).** This is a concrete avoidance rule — Bb must not rest on a chord tone but rather approach one — a critical guideline for idiomatic phrasing over the substitution.

## Mixolydian mode option

> The fourth option to solo with a tritone
>
> sub is to use the Mixolydian mode (1 - 2 . .
> -3-4-5-6-b7). Ithas the same note as Mixolydian Mode Shapes
>
> the major pentatonic scale with a fourth  ~—-@ @ rp 5) ©
> (4/11) and a seventh (7).

**Significance (p. 2).** Completes the four-option framework by defining Mixolydian and relating it to the already-known pentatonic — a pedagogical bridge that reduces the cognitive load of learning the new option.

## Gb and B treatment rules (Mixolydian)

> The seventh of Db7 (B) is the third of G7.

**Significance (p. 2).** Gives two specific rules about which scale degrees to avoid as resting tones and which to target, shaping how Mixolydian is applied in practice.

## Lick #1 bar-by-bar construction

> Bar 1: Dm7 arpeggio (D - F - A - C)
> Bar 2 : Descending Db7 arpeggio (Db - F - A - Cb)
> Bar 3 : CM7 arpeggio (C - E- G- B)

**Significance (p. 2).** This is the first fully notated example applying option one across all three bars of the II V I, showing exactly how arpeggio choices are assigned measure-by-measure.

## Lick #2: Db pentatonic tone analysis

> Playing Db pentatonic over G7 chord provides some interesting colors:
> Db is the #11 of the G7 chord.
> Eb is the b13 of G7.
> F is the minor 7th of G7.
> Ab is the b9 of G7.

**Significance (p. 3).** Provides a complete tone-by-tone analysis of Db major pentatonic against G7, giving the student a reusable reference for why this scale sounds idiomatic over an altered dominant.

## Lick #3: Db9 arpeggio tone analysis

> Playing Db9 arpeggio on G7 highlights the following tones :
>
> Db is the #11 of the G7 chord.
>
> F is the minor 7th of G7.
> Ab is the b9 of G7.
> Cb is the major third of G7.
> Eb is the b13 of G7.

**Significance (p. 3).** Extends the tone-mapping method to the dom9 arpeggio, reinforcing the analytical framework across a second option so students can generalize the approach.

## Lick #5: chromaticism to resolve

> Measure 2 : Db9 arpeggio (Eb - Cb - Ab - F - Db) and chromaticism to approach the root of CM7.

**Significance (p. 4).** Explicitly names chromaticism as a device used to connect the tritone-sub bar to the tonic resolution, teaching a specific voice-leading technique.

## Dorian bebop scale definition

> Measure 1 : D Dorian bebop (D - E - F - F# - G- A- B- C). The Dorian bebop scale is the Dorian mode with
>
> a passing tone.

**Significance (p. 5).** Defines the Dorian bebop scale by specifying its formula and explaining that F# is a chromatic passing tone added to Dorian — a distinct scale concept introduced only in the final lick.

## Lick #6: Db7 Mixolydian application

> Measure 2 : Db7 mixolydian mode ( Db - Eb - F - Gb - Ab - Bb - Cb).

**Significance (p. 5).** Demonstrates option four (Mixolydian) in a concrete lick context, completing the cycle so every one of the four options introduced earlier has been illustrated in a musical example.
